excerpt: "Who picks up the phone when a transaction goes wrong?" Closing out Toronto Tech week last Friday, I had the chance to speak at the Digital Asset Summit by Web3 Toronto (Web3TO) and one thing I noticed is that the anxiety around stablecoins is mostly misplaced. What stablecoins actually enable is private issuers competing to provide better banking services to consumers. That competition is good. It drives down fees, improves user experience, and forces innovation that incumbents are not incentivized enough to pursue. The trust question answers itself. Nobody holds a stablecoin issued by someone they don't trust with their money. The market solves this. On retail CBDCs, I keep coming back to a simple question: who picks up the phone when something goes wrong? With a bank payment, you call your bank. With a stablecoin, you call the issuer. Both have someone accountable on the other end. With a retail CBDC, who exactly is serving the user? The central bank is not set up to handle retail customer complaints at scale. The infrastructure required to support millions of retail users is enormous...and the privacy concerns are real. Wholesale CBDCs have a stronger use case because they are a more efficient means of intraday settlement than current high speed rails. There may be overlap between wholesale CBDCs and tokenized deposits given that their use cases are mostly inter-FI settlements. Tokenized deposits are a different instrument for a different purpose. Their real value is in how banks move money amongst each other and in domestic payment rails. That distinction matters and it gets lost in most conversations that treat all digital money as the same thing. The bigger point underneath all of this: tokenized money is the future of financial infrastructure because it replaces dinosaur technology that has no business running a 21st century financial system. - Thank you to Doug Heintzman, Blockchain Research Institute for moderating along with fellow panelists: Peter MacKenzie, PhD, C.D. Howe Institute, Kesem Frank, Stablecorp Inc.
